title: End of module: Particles and Quantum Phenomena

questions.text: What is the quark composition of a proton?
questions.explanation: The proton is uud: charge $= \frac{2}{3}e + \frac{2}{3}e - \frac{1}{3}e = +1e$, matching the proton's known charge.

questions.text: Using quark charges up $= +\frac{2}{3}e$ and down $= -\frac{1}{3}e$, calculate the total charge of a neutron (udd), in units of $e$.
questions.explanation: $\frac{2}{3}e - \frac{1}{3}e - \frac{1}{3}e = 0$, consistent with the neutron being electrically neutral.

questions.text: Which fundamental force holds quarks together inside protons and neutrons, and has a range of about $10^{-15}\,\text{m}$?
questions.explanation: The strong nuclear force acts over nuclear-sized distances ($\sim 10^{-15}\,\text{m}$) and is what binds quarks into hadrons and nucleons into nuclei.

questions.text: Which of the following quantities must be conserved in every particle interaction or decay?
choices: Kinetic energy of each individual particle
questions.explanation: Charge, baryon number and lepton number are all conserved overall. Total energy (not each particle's kinetic energy alone) is conserved — kinetic energy can be converted to/from mass-energy or other forms.

questions.text: Calculate the energy, in joules, of a photon of frequency $5.00 \times 10^{14}\,\text{Hz}$. Use $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J s}$.
A metal has work function $\Phi = 2.30\,\text{eV}$. Using $h = 6.63\times10^{-34}\,\text{J s}$ and $e = 1.60\times10^{-19}\,\text{C}$, calculate its threshold frequency $f_0$ in Hz.
Light of frequency $8.00\times10^{14}\,\text{Hz}$ shines on a metal with work function $\Phi = 2.30\,\text{eV}$. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons, in eV.
questions.explanation: $E_{\text{photon}} = hf = (6.63\times10^{-34})(8.00\times10^{14}) = 5.304\times10^{-19}\,\text{J} = 3.315\,\text{eV}$. Then $KE_{\max} = 3.315 - 2.30 = 1.015\,\text{eV}$ by Einstein's photoelectric equation.

questions.text: Light with a frequency below a metal's threshold frequency is shone on the metal, and its intensity is then increased. What happens?
choices: Photoemission occurs with a higher maximum kinetic energy
Photoemission occurs with more photoelectrons per second, but the same maximum kinetic energy
No photoemission occurs at all, regardless of the intensity
Photoemission occurs, but only after a short time delay
questions.explanation: Each photon below the threshold frequency carries too little energy ($hf < \Phi$) to free an electron. Increasing the intensity only increases the number of photons per second, not the energy of each one, so no electron is ever emitted.

questions.text: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of an electron (mass $9.11\times10^{-31}\,\text{kg}$) travelling at $2.00\times10^{6}\,\text{m s}^{-1}$. Use $h = 6.63\times10^{-34}\,\text{J s}$.
Which experimental observation provides direct evidence that electrons have wave-like properties?
choices: Electron diffraction through a thin crystalline sample
The existence of atomic emission line spectra
questions.explanation: Electron diffraction produces a pattern of rings just as light does through a diffraction grating — only a wave can diffract, so this confirms electrons have wave-like character, as predicted by de Broglie.

questions.text: In hydrogen, $E_n = -13.6/n^2\,\text{eV}$. Calculate the energy, in eV, of the photon emitted when an electron falls from $n=3$ to $n=2$.
What name is given to a single, discrete packet (quantum) of electromagnetic radiation, of energy $E = hf$?
questions.explanation: A discrete quantum of electromagnetic radiation is called a **photon**.
